premise.title: Rietveld/Le Bail fits produce space-group assignments
premise.name: rietveld_le_bail_spacegroup_assignment

premises.premise: Rietveld and Le Bail quantitative powder diffraction refinement methods are applied to synchrotron X-ray powder diffraction data collected from compressed samples of yttrium hydride phases; the data were collected with incident X-ray wavelengths $ \lambda = 0.2952\ \text{\AA} $ and $ \lambda = 0.3344\ \text{\AA} $, integrated to one-dimensional intensity versus $2\theta$ patterns, and fit by Rietveld/Le Bail procedures that return refined unit-cell lattice parameters, atomic coordinates for yttrium atoms, phase weight fractions and calculated diffraction peak positions and intensities; the claim is that these Rietveld/Le Bail fits uniquely justify assigning the observed diffraction patterns to the space groups Fm$\bar{3}$m (fcc YH$_{3}$), I4/mmm (bct YH$_{4}$), Im$\bar{3}$m (bcc YH$_{6}$) and P6$_{3}$/mmc (hcp YH$_{9}$) because the fitted peak positions and intensities for those space groups align with the experimental peak positions and intensities and the refinements returned sensible phase weight fractions and atomic coordinates consistent with the fits. [57,58] Fig. 1

premise.title: Vinet equation-of-state fits describe V(P) over 135–410 GPa
premise.name: vinet_eos_fit_validity

premises.premise: The pressure dependence of unit-cell volume $V$ for each identified yttrium hydride phase (where $V$ is the crystallographic unit-cell volume in cubic angstroms, measured from powder X-ray diffraction at each pressure) is parametrized by fitting the measured $V(P)$ data over the approximate pressure interval $P\in[135\ \mathrm{GPa},410\ \mathrm{GPa}]$ to the Vinet empirical equation of state (where the Vinet equation is the empirical relation $P(V)=3B_{0}\left(1-x\right)x^{-2}\exp\left[\tfrac{3}{2}\left(B_{0}'-1\right)\left(1-x\right)\right]$ with $x=(V/V_{0})^{1/3}$, bulk modulus $B_{0}$ and its pressure derivative $B_{0}'$), and the claim is that these Vinet fits provide accurate continuous interpolations/extrapolations of $V(P)$ for each phase across the stated pressure range that were used subsequently to compute derived quantities such as hydrogen-induced volume expansion per hydrogen atom. Fig. 2

premise.title: Hydrogen-induced volume expansion method yields composition estimates
premise.name: hydrogen_induced_volume_composition_estimate

premises.premise: Define the hydrogen-induced volume expansion per hydrogen (or deuterium) atom as $V_{H}$ in units of \AA$^{3}$/H(D) atom; using (a) the experimentally measured unit-cell volumes $V(P)$ for an identified yttrium hydride phase at pressure $P$, (b) an extrapolated equation of state for pure elemental yttrium giving $V_{\mathrm{Y}}(P)$ (where $V_{\mathrm{Y}}(P)$ is the unit-cell volume per Y atom at pressure $P$ for the pure metal), and (c) the relation $V_{H}(P)=\dfrac{V(P)-V_{\mathrm{Y}}(P)}{n_{\mathrm{H}}}$ where $n_{\mathrm{H}}$ is the number of H(D) atoms per Y atom in the assumed stoichiometry, the authors computed $V_{H}$ and inverted this relation to estimate the hydrogen content $n_{\mathrm{H}}$ for the hydrogen-richer phases; the specific claim is that this $V_{H}$-based procedure yields composition estimates $YH_{5.7(3)}$ for the bcc-like phase and $YH_{8.5(5)}$ for the hcp-like phase, with the stated uncertainties in the final digit. [33] Fig. 2

premise.title: Assignment of superconducting transitions to XRD-identified phases
premise.name: phase_assignment_of_resistive_transitions

premises.premise: Electrical resistance versus temperature transitions measured in a four-probe van der Pauw geometry (where four sputtered leads arranged on the diamond anvil contact the sample and DC currents in the range $10^{-5}\text{–}10^{-3}\,$A are applied and the sample temperature is measured with a Si diode thermometer of $\sim1\,$K accuracy) are attributed to specific crystallographic phases (Im$\bar{3}$m bcc YH$_{6}$/YD$_{6}$ or P6$_{3}$/mmc hcp YH$_{9}$/YD$_{9}$) by correlating (i) the temperatures of the observed resistive drops with (ii) X-ray diffraction-determined phase content and spatial phase distribution in the same sample; the claim is that, based on these correlations and the observation that impurity lower-hydride phases (e.g., fcc-YH$_{3}$ and bct-YH$_{4}$) appear only in poorly heated regions near electrical leads and are expected to have much lower $T_{c}$, the observed high-temperature resistive transitions can be assigned to the bcc-YH$_{6}$ and hcp-YH$_{9}$ phases rather than to impurity phases. Fig. 4

premise.title: Onset criterion and measurement accuracy determine T_c
premise.name: tc_onset_criterion_and_temperature_accuracy

premises.premise: The superconducting critical temperature $T_{c}$ in resistance measurements is defined operationally as the onset temperature at which the measured electrical resistance $R(T)$ upon warming first deviates from the extrapolated normal-state metallic behaviour, where temperature is recorded by an Si diode thermometer attached to the diamond anvil cell with an estimated accuracy of $\sim1\,$K and the warming rate is sufficiently slow (e.g., $\sim0.2\,$K min$^{-1}$) to reduce thermal lag; the claim is that using this onset criterion and the stated thermometer accuracy yields the reported numerical $T_{c}$ values. [Methods]

premise.title: Measurement sensitivity sufficient to detect superconductivity in lower hydrides
premise.name: measurement_sensitivity_for_lower_hydrides

premises.premise: DC four-probe electrical resistance measurements with applied currents in the $10^{-5}\text{–}10^{-3}\,$A range and temperature control and measurement down to $5\,$K for pressures in the reported ranges (15–180 GPa for fcc-YH$_{3}$ and down to $78\,$K at 250 GPa for bct-YH$_{4}$) are sufficiently sensitive to detect a superconducting resistive transition (a sharp drop of resistance to zero or a pronounced onset deviation from metallic resistance) if a bulk superconducting phase with a transition temperature above the stated experimental lower-temperature bounds were present and electrically connected to the measurement leads. [Methods] Supplementary Fig. 4; Fig. 2

premise.title: Paired hydride/deuteride T_c measurements for isotope coefficient
premise.name: paired_hydride_deuteride_tc_measurements

premises.premise: Define the isotope-effect coefficient $\alpha$ by $\alpha = -\dfrac{d\ln T_{c}}{d\ln M}$ where $M$ is the atomic mass of the hydrogen isotope (i.e., $M\approx1.0079\,$u for H and $M\approx2.0141\,$u for D); the claim is that superconducting transition temperatures $T_{c}$ were measured for closely matched hydride and deuteride samples (YH$_{6}$/YD$_{6}$ and YH$_{9}$/YD$_{9}$) under the same or directly comparable pressure conditions in the $183\text{–}205\,$GPa range, and that these paired $T_{c}$ data were used to compute the numerical isotope coefficients $\alpha\approx0.39$ for the bcc phase and $\alpha\approx0.50$ for the hcp phase. Fig. 4

premise.title: Interpreting isotope exponent as evidence for phonon-mediated pairing
premise.name: isotope_exponent_interpreted_as_phonon_pairing

premises.premise: Within the conventional Bardeen–Cooper–Schrieffer (BCS) and Migdal–Eliashberg frameworks for phonon-mediated superconductivity, the isotopic-mass dependence of the superconducting critical temperature leads to an isotope-effect exponent $\alpha=0.5$ in the ideal harmonic limit (i.e., if phonon frequencies scale as $M^{-1/2}$ and pairing is entirely phonon-mediated in the harmonic approximation); the claim is that measured isotope exponents of $\alpha\approx0.39$ (bcc-YH$_{6}$/YD$_{6}$) and $\alpha\approx0.50$ (hcp-YH$_{9}$/YD$_{9}$) are evidence supporting a predominantly phonon-mediated pairing mechanism, with the reduced value $\alpha\approx0.39$ interpreted as indicating additional effects such as anharmonic phonon renormalization or acoustic-phonon contributions to the electron–phonon coupling. [1] Fig. 4

premise.title: WHH/GL fits extrapolate H_c2(0) from 9 T data
premise.name: whh_gl_extrapolation_from_9T

premises.premise: Resistance-versus-temperature data collected at multiple fixed external magnetic fields up to $9\,$T are used to determine $T_{c}(H)$ points (using a 90% of normal-state resistance criterion), and the Werthamer–Helfand–Hohenberg (WHH) model and Ginzburg–Landau (GL) relation are fit to the measured $H_{c2}(T)$ data; the claim is that fitting these models to data limited to $H\le 9\,$T and extrapolating the fits yields reliable estimates of the zero-temperature upper critical field $H_{c2}(0)$ for the studied samples, despite the large extrapolation in field relative to the fitted range. [44,45] Fig. 5

premise.title: Coherence length formula valid for H_c2 extraction
premise.name: coherence_length_formula_validity

premises.premise: The superconducting coherence length $\xi$ at a given temperature is related to the upper critical field $H_{c2}$ by the formula $\xi = \sqrt{\dfrac{\phi_{0}}{3\pi^{2}H_{c2}}}$ where $\phi_{0}=h/2e$ is the magnetic flux quantum (with $h$ Planck's constant and $e$ the elementary charge); the claim is that using this formula with the experimentally extracted $H_{c2}$ values provides reasonable estimates of the superconducting coherence length $\xi(0)$ for the studied yttrium hydride samples.

premise.title: Use of BCS xi–v_F relation to estimate Fermi velocity
premise.name: bcs_xi_vf_relation_applicability

premises.premise: The Bardeen–Cooper–Schrieffer (BCS) relation $\xi = 0.18\,\dfrac{h v_{F}}{k_{\mathrm{B}} T_{c}}$ (where $\xi$ is the superconducting coherence length at zero temperature, $h$ is Planck's constant, $v_{F}$ is the Fermi velocity, $k_{\mathrm{B}}$ is Boltzmann's constant, and $T_{c}$ is the superconducting critical temperature) is employed to estimate a characteristic Fermi velocity $v_{F}$ by inserting experimentally determined $\xi(0)$ and $T_{c}$ values; the claim is that this single-band BCS-derived relation yields reasonable order-of-magnitude estimates of $v_{F}$ for these hydride superconductors. Fig. 5

premise.title: Scaling H_c2 by (T_c/T2)^2 and equal vF assumption
premise.name: hc2_scaling_and_equal_vf_assumption

premises.premise: To estimate the zero-temperature upper critical field $H_{c2}(0)$ of the hydrogenated P6$_{3}$/mmc YH$_{9}$ phase from the measured deuteride P6$_{3}$/mmc YD$_{9}$ data, a scaling relation $H_{c2}\sim\left(\dfrac{T_{c}}{T_{2}}\right)^{2}$ is applied (where $T_{c}$ denotes the superconducting temperature of the hydride and $T_{2}$ that of the deuteride) together with the assumption that the Fermi velocity $v_{F}$ is comparable between hydride and deuteride counterparts; the claim is that this combined scaling and equal-$v_{F}$ assumption yields an approximate $H_{c2}^{\mathrm{WHH}}(0)\approx120\,$T for hcp-YH$_{9}$. Fig. 5

premise.title: XRD detection sensitivity would reveal fcc‑YH10 if present
premise.name: xrd_sensitivity_to_yh10

premises.premise: During pulsed-laser heating experiments up to reported peak temperatures (e.g., $2250(10)\,$K) at pressures up to $410\,$GPa, time-resolved in situ powder X-ray diffraction patterns were collected by accumulating $5\times10^{5}$ laser-synchronized exposures per pattern with the synchrotron beam spot and detector setup described, and the claim is that these in situ and post-quench X-ray diffraction measurements have sufficient angular coverage, resolution and signal-to-noise to detect the formation of an fcc-YH$_{10}$ phase (i.e., diffraction peaks at the predicted fcc-YH$_{10}$ lattice parameter would have been observable) if that phase appeared as a substantial or transient crystalline product within the illuminated sample volume during heating or after quench. Fig. 3

premise.title: Sample 17 realized excess hydrogen and formed YH9
premise.name: sample17_excess_hydrogen_realized

premises.premise: In a specific experimental run identified as sample 17, a starting assembly composed of YH$_{3}$ mixed with the hydrogen source NH$_{3}$BH$_{3}$ was laser-heated at $410\,$GPa and $2250(10)\,$K and the observed outcome by post-heating X-ray diffraction was transformation to single-phase P6$_{3}$/mmc YH$_{9}$ (i.e., the XRD pattern after quench matches the hcp-YH$_{9}$ phase with no detectable fcc-YH$_{10}$ reflections); the claim is that this observation demonstrates that hydrogen-rich conditions sufficient to form a high-hydrogen stoichiometry were achieved in at least this experiment despite the experimental challenge of controlling excess H$_{2}$ when using NH$_{3}$BH$_{3}$. Fig. 3

premise.title: Measured maximum T_c values reflect intrinsic phase properties
premise.name: measured_tc_intrinsic_to_phases

premises.premise: The reported maximum superconducting critical temperatures (numerical maxima of $T_{c}\approx224\,$K for bcc-YH$_{6}$ and $T_{c}=243\,$K for hcp-YH$_{9}$ as reported in the experiments) are taken to represent intrinsic superconducting properties of the corresponding crystallographic phases under the stated pressure conditions, i.e., the claim is that these maximal $T_{c}$ values are not predominantly suppressed by sample inhomogeneity, grain boundary disorder, poor crystallinity, incomplete phase purity or measurement artifacts such that they would be substantially higher in idealized, perfectly crystalline bulk samples of the same phases under identical thermodynamic conditions.

premise.title: Dome-shaped T_c(P) profiles reflect intrinsic pressure dependence
premise.name: dome_shape_intrinsic_not_artifact

premises.premise: The observed pressure dependence of superconducting critical temperature $T_{c}(P)$ for the Im$\bar{3}$m YH$_{6}$ and P6$_{3}$/mmc YH$_{9}$ phases, showing an initial increase of $T_{c}$ with pressure to a maximum followed by a decrease at higher pressures (a dome-like profile), reflects an intrinsic dependence of the superconducting pairing strength or lattice dynamics on pressure in those phases rather than being an experimental artifact arising from pressure gradients, phase coexistence, or misassignment of phase identity across the pressure series. Fig. 4

contexts.context: Pressure values reported in this study are estimated either from the frequency of the $H_{2}$ (or $D_{2}$) vibron observed in Raman spectra (denoted $P_{H}$ or $P_{D}$) or from the high-frequency edge of the stressed-diamond Raman line (diamond scale), and it is noted that the diamond-based scale typically overestimates pressure relative to the vibron-based scale by approximately $5\text{–}40\,$GPa due to pressure gradients between the hydrogen medium and the gasket; this contextual uncertainty affects absolute pressure placement of data points but is not asserted to invalidate phase identifications. [54,55]

context.title: Anomalous V_H in YH3 compared with higher hydrides
context.name: anomalous_volume_expansion_in_yh3

contexts.context: The hydrogen-induced volume expansion per hydrogen atom $V_{H}$ in YH$_{3}$ is experimentally observed to be anomalously large compared with $V_{H}$ measured in higher yttrium hydrides (i.e., the derived $V_{H}$ values are not constant across stoichiometries), and as a result some phases (bct-YH$_{4}$ and fcc-YH$_{3}$) exhibit similar lattice volumes in the 150–250 GPa pressure range; this context motivates careful interpretation of $V_{H}$-based composition estimates. Fig. 2

context.title: Impurities located near leads and their expected low T_c
context.name: impurities_near_leads_low_tc

contexts.context: Lower-hydrogen impurity phases such as fcc-YH$_{3}$ and bct-YH$_{4}$ were observed in X-ray diffraction patterns in regions spatially proximate to electrical leads that were intentionally kept poorly heated; these impurity phases are expected on theoretical grounds and prior experiments to have much lower superconducting critical temperatures than the high-$T_{c}$ transitions reported for bcc-YH$_{6}$ and hcp-YH$_{9}$, and therefore they are treated as unlikely sources of the observed high-$T_{c}$ resistive transitions. Fig. 4

context.title: Experimental difficulty controlling excess H2 when using NH3BH3
context.name: difficulty_controlling_excess_hydrogen

contexts.context: When NH$_{3}$BH$_{3}$ is used as an in situ hydrogen source in a diamond anvil cell, controlling the absolute amount of excess molecular hydrogen $H_{2}$ generated during heating is experimentally challenging, so realization of a precisely defined hydrogen chemical potential in such experiments is uncertain and may vary sample-to-sample.

conclusions.conclusion: Given that the underlying X-ray diffraction data were collected with high-energy synchrotron radiation and were fit using standard Rietveld/Le Bail procedures and that the Vinet fits and $V_{H}$ analyses are internally consistent with prior theoretical structure predictions, the reasoning that the synthesized phases are Fm$\bar{3}$m (fcc) YH$_{3$,} I4/mmm (bct) YH$_{4}$, Im$\bar{3}$m (bcc) YH$_{6}$ and P6$_{3}$/mmc (hcp) YH$_{9}$ and that the hydrogen-richer phases have composition estimates near YH$_{5.7(3)}$ and YH$_{8.5(5)}$ is methodologically sound provided the refinement and composition-estimation premises (P1–P3) hold.

Assuming the phase identifications from XRD are correct (conclusion 1) and that the attribution of resistive transitions to particular phases via spatial/phase-fraction correlation is valid (premise P4) and that $T_{c}$ values are determined with the stated onset criterion and temperature accuracy (premise P5), the logical inference that Im$\bar{3}$m bcc YH$_{6}$ and P6$_{3}$/mmc hcp YH$_{9}$ are superconducting with dome-shaped $T_{c}(P)$ dependences and the reported maximum $T_{c}$ values is convincing; remaining uncertainties concern sample-to-sample variation and precise pressure placement rather than the internal logic of the attribution. [@conclusion-1]

Given the XRD-based phase identifications (conclusion 1) and the decompression experiments that showed appearance/disappearance of diffraction peaks concomitant with changes in transport, the reported decomposition pressures (e.g., hcp-YH$_{9}\to$ bcc-YH$_{6}$ at $\sim159\,$GPa, and bcc-YH$_{6}$ decomposing at $\sim135\,$GPa in a deuteride) are credible experimental observations; interpretation that these experimental decomposition pressures exceed some theoretical equilibrium formation pressures (implying hysteresis/kinetic effects) is plausible but more interpretive. [@conclusion-1]

Provided the DC four-probe measurements had the sensitivity and temperature range stated (premise P8) and the XRD phase identification in measured samples is accurate (conclusion 1), the negative experimental finding of no superconducting transition in fcc-YH$_{3}$ down to 5 K (15–180 GPa) and in bct-YH$_{4}$ down to 78 K at 250 GPa is a reliable empirical constraint that contradicts some theoretical predictions for those stoichiometries within the explored temperature windows; the conclusion is limited to the stated measurement windows. [@conclusion-1]

Assuming the paired hydride/deuteride $T_{c}$ measurements were performed on comparable samples and pressures (premise P9) and that the interpretation of isotope exponents as diagnostic of phonon-mediated pairing is applicable (premise P10), the reasoning that the observed isotope-effect coefficients ($\alpha\approx0.39$ for YH$_{6}$ and $\alpha\approx0.50$ for YH$_{9}$) support a predominantly phonon-mediated superconducting mechanism is coherent and consistent with conventional theoretical expectations, with the caveat that reduced $\alpha$ can reflect anharmonicity or other nonidealities. [@conclusion-1], [@conclusion-2]

Given the magneto-transport $T_{c}(H)$ data up to $9\,$T and the applied analysis procedures, the chain of reasoning to fit GL and WHH models (premise P11), extract $H_{c2}(0)$, compute coherence lengths via $\xi=\sqrt{\dfrac{\phi_{0}}{3\pi^{2}H_{c2}}}$ (premise P12), and then estimate Fermi velocities using the BCS relation $\xi = 0.18\,\dfrac{h v_{F}}{k_{\mathrm{B}} T_{c}}$ (premise P13) is straightforward but contains substantial extrapolation and model-assumption uncertainties; consequently the qualitative conclusion (short coherence lengths and large upper critical fields) is well supported, while the specific numerical $H_{c2}(0)$ and $v_{F}$ estimates carry notable model-dependent uncertainty (particularly due to extrapolation beyond the measured 9 T range and simplifying assumptions such as single-band BCS applicability and the scaling/equal-$v_{F}$ assumption used for hydrogenated vs deuterated estimates). [@conclusion-1], [@conclusion-2]

Assuming the in situ and post-quench X-ray diffraction experiments had the sensitivity and sampling described (premise P15) and that at least one trial achieved hydrogen-rich conditions (premise P16), the negative empirical result that fcc-YH$_{10}$ was not observed within the authors' explored pressure–temperature window up to $410\,$GPa and $2250\,$K is a robust experimental constraint on the realizability of the theoretically predicted fcc-YH$_{10}$ phase under those laboratory-accessible conditions; this conclusion does not rule out fcc-YH$_{10}$ at substantially different P–T or kinetic pathways beyond those explored.

If the reported maximal experimental $T_{c}$ values for bcc-YH$_{6}$ and hcp-YH$_{9}$ are representative of the intrinsic phase behaviour under the measured conditions (premise P17) and the cited theoretical predictions are being compared to the same thermodynamic variables, then the observation that measured $T_{c}$ values are systematically lower by roughly $20\text{–}30\,$K than many prior theoretical predictions is a valid empirical conclusion that motivates further theoretical work incorporating effects beyond approximations used in those predictions (e.g., stronger anharmonicity, finite-temperature free energies, beyond-Migdal corrections); residual uncertainty remains about the extent to which sample quality or experimental conditions may suppress $T_{c}$ relative to idealized theoretical models. [@conclusion-2]

Assuming the phase-resolved $T_{c}(P)$ datasets are accurate and representative (conclusion 2 and premise P18), the reported dome-like pressure dependences of $T_{c}$ for Im$\bar{3}$m YH$_{6}$ and P6$_{3}$/mmc YH$_{9}$ (with maxima near the reported pressures and subsequent decreases at higher pressures) are well supported; the interpretation that the decrease of $T_{c}$ at higher pressures is consistent with pressure-induced phonon stiffening or electronic-structure changes is plausible and aligns with standard mechanisms, although detailed theoretical analysis is required to discriminate among mechanisms. [@conclusion-2]
